core_identity.title: Using Trigonometry to Find Missing Sides
core_identity.author: Gerry Docherty

curriculum_alignment.subject: mathematics
curriculum_alignment.subtopic: trig ratios to find a missing side
curriculum_alignment.seeds_of_change: Select and apply sin, cos, or tan (SOH-CAH-TOA) to calculate unknown side lengths in right-angled triangles, explaining why the chosen ratio fits the given information.

verse_ality.driving_question: What is always true about SOH-CAH-TOA, and how can these ratios help us calculate unknown sides in right-angled triangles?

knowing_world.focus: Use SOH-CAH-TOA to connect angles to side lengths and calculate a missing side in a right-angled triangle (given one angle and one side).
key_concepts: Opposite, adjacent, hypotenuse (relative to the chosen angle)
Choosing the correct ratio based on known/unknown sides
Rearranging trig equations to find a missing side
tools: Video: Trig Ratios - Finding a Missing Side (canva_embed): https://www.canva.com/design/DAGu0GYyHbY/MkVHrJZZ8WhQG_bGXXVMfw/watch?embed

human_story.focus: Connect trigonometry to real work in surveying, engineering, architecture, aviation, and robotics—measuring heights, distances, and slopes when direct measurement is not possible.
justice_prompts: Where does this matter in everyday life? Who depends on us getting these calculations right?

expressive_self.focus: Make personal meaning of triangle logic by representing and explaining sin, cos, and tan in a way that helps you remember and apply them.
activities: Draw or annotate a triangle from memory showing clear SOH-CAH-TOA labels.
Explain the difference between sin, cos, and tan in your own words (e.g., a short poem, cartoon, or mini-explainer).
Reflect: What feels easy when solving trig problems? What feels tricky?
Choose a learning metaphor that helps the idea ‘click’ for you.

designed_world.focus: Use an interactive sketch to generate triangle scenarios, calculate missing sides, and consider how to design measurement tools that work for everyone.
design_challenges: Use the sketch to calculate a missing side length given an angle and one side.
Drag point C to create different triangle scenarios and solve each one.
Challenge a classmate: create a triangle and ask them to find the missing side; then verify together.
Ethical design: adapt a triangle-measuring interface for visually impaired users or someone with fine motor challenges.

regulation_supports: Pause after a set of problems: trace a triangle shape in the air with your fingers.
Breathe in for 3 and out for 4; imagine your triangle rotating as you breathe.
Stretch like two triangle sides meeting, then release neck and shoulder tension.
wellbeing_prompts: How can you help your body reset when a problem gets frustrating?

pathways.name: Final Task: Triangle Toolkit (SDG 4 – Quality Education)
pathways.focus: Design a mini ‘toolkit’ that teaches others how to use trig ratios to find missing sides, using multiple examples and clear reasoning for ratio choice.
products: Three triangle examples (one using sin, one using cos, one using tan) built in a web sketch tool, with screenshots.
For each triangle: written instructions explaining which ratio to use and why.
A short guide/tutorial for one triangle (text, audio, or recorded walkthrough).
Self and peer assessment using the GCSE Maths Rubric: Conceptual Understanding, Fluency & Accuracy, Application to Problems, Independence & Reflection.

required: Identify opposite, adjacent and hypotenuse relative to a chosen angle in a right-angled triangle
Use a calculator to evaluate sin, cos and tan of an angle (in degrees)
Rearrange simple equations to make a different subject

builds_toward: Using trigonometry to find missing angles
Problem solving with bearings and elevation/depression
